title: Stripe endpoint secrets should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Stripe webhook endpoint secret detected in source code. Webhook endpoint secrets (whsec_*) are critical credentials used to verify webhook signatures from Stripe, ensuring that incoming webhook events are genuinely from Stripe and not malicious actors attempting to inject fake payment events. These secrets enable secure event-driven integrations for payment processing, subscription management, dispute notifications, refund confirmations, customer lifecycle events, and financial reconciliation workflows. Exposing webhook secrets creates severe security risks: attackers can forge webhook events to trigger unauthorized actions, inject fake payment confirmations to bypass actual payment verification, create fraudulent subscription activations without valid charges, manipulate refund and payout notifications, impersonate successful payments for order fulfillment, trigger invoice generation for unpaid services, modify customer billing data through webhook spoofing, bypass payment fraud detection by injecting approved transaction events, exhaust webhook processing resources with DoS attacks, gain intelligence about business operations and payment volumes, exploit race conditions in event processing, and compromise financial audit trails with falsified transaction records. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS REQUIRED: 1) Rotate the exposed webhook secret immediately via Stripe Dashboard (Developers → Webhooks → Endpoint → Signing secret → Roll secret), 2) Update all webhook endpoint configurations with the new secret across all applications and services, 3) Review webhook event logs for suspicious or unauthorized webhook deliveries during the exposure period (unusual event patterns, events from unexpected IP addresses, timing anomalies, duplicate events, or events for non-existent resources), 4) Audit all actions triggered by webhook events (orders fulfilled, subscriptions activated, access granted, refunds issued, credits applied) during the exposure window, 5) Verify financial reconciliation for all webhook-driven transactions, 6) Implement webhook signature verification in all webhook handlers using Stripe's SDK libraries (stripe.webhooks.constructEvent() for Node.js, Stripe\Webhook::constructEvent() for PHP, stripe.Webhook.construct_event() for Python), 7) Store webhook secrets in secure secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager) with appropriate access controls, 8) Never commit webhook secrets to version control, documentation, or configuration files, 9) Use separate webhook endpoints and secrets for different environments (development, staging, production), 10) Implement webhook endpoint authentication beyond signature verification (IP allowlisting, rate limiting, request validation), 11) Add monitoring and alerting for webhook signature verification failures, unusual event volumes, or processing errors, 12) Enable webhook event retention in Stripe Dashboard for forensic analysis, 13) Consider using Stripe Connect for multi-tenant applications to isolate webhook secrets per connected account, 14) Document when and where the secret was exposed for compliance and incident response records, 15) Review access logs and commit history to identify how the secret was exposed and prevent recurrence.

Stripe webhook endpoint secret detected in variable assignment. This webhook secret (whsec_*) is used to cryptographically verify that webhook events originate from Stripe's servers, preventing malicious webhook injection attacks. Compromise of webhook secrets allows attackers to forge payment events, bypass transaction verification, manipulate subscription states, inject fraudulent notifications, and compromise financial workflows. CRITICAL REMEDIATION: 1) Rotate webhook secret immediately via Stripe Dashboard (Webhooks → Roll secret), 2) Update all applications with new secret, 3) Audit webhook event logs for unauthorized deliveries, 4) Review all webhook-triggered actions during exposure period (fulfilled orders, activated subscriptions, granted access, processed refunds), 5) Verify financial reconciliation for webhook-driven transactions, 6) Implement proper webhook signature verification using Stripe SDK methods (constructEvent, construct_event), 7) Store secrets in vault systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault), 8) Never commit webhook secrets to repositories, 9) Use separate secrets per environment (dev/staging/prod), 10) Add monitoring for verification failures and suspicious webhook patterns, 11) Implement rate limiting and IP allowlisting for webhook endpoints, 12) Enable webhook event retention for forensics, 13) Document exposure for compliance records, 14) Review how secret was exposed to prevent recurrence.
